text: The video shows the standard port positions for laparoscopic posterior exenteration, with ports strategically placed for optimal access to the pelvic region.
The surgeon uses an open technique for port insertion at the supraumbilical position. Due to the patient's umbilical hernia, primary access is gained through a port in the right iliac fossa.
The omentum is visibly herniating through the umbilical defect, showing protrusion of the fatty tissue through the abdominal wall.
The surgeon removes the omentum from the hernial sac, allowing for the insertion of the umbilical port.
The surgeon retracts the sigmoid colon ventrally and incises the medial peritoneal fold, exposing the underlying anatomical structures.
The surgeon employs a medial to lateral approach, continuing dissection in the avascular plane while carefully avoiding injury to the left ureter.
The surgeon dissects the inferior mesenteric artery at its origin, clips it, and divides it, ensuring clear separation from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon divides the infirm eccentric vein, completing the dissection of this vascular structure.
The surgeon incises the lateral peritoneal fold and continues dissection cranially to mobilize the splenic flexure, revealing the anatomical structures involved in the procedure.
The surgeon performs major dissection in the avascular presacral plane, extending the dissection down to the pelvic floor.
The surgeon makes a lateral peritoneal incision in the pelvis and skeletonizes the fundibulopelvic ligament, which is then clipped and divided.
The surgeon develops the paravesical and pararectal spaces, further advancing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon dissects the uterine artery from the ureter, clipping it at its origin from the internal iliac artery, visually separating the paravacycle and pararectal spaces.
The surgeon performs further dissection of the ureter, completely mobilizing it up to its insertion at the bladder.
The surgeon clips and divides the round ligament in the pelvic region, following the previous dissection of the fundibulopelvic ligament.
The surgeon repeats the dissection procedure on the right side, developing the paravacicle and pararectal spaces and mobilizing the ureter up to the bladder insertion.
The surgeon makes an incision on the uterovisceral fold of the peritoneum, separating the bladder from the lower uterine segment and cervix.
The surgeon performs dissection over the McCartney tube circumferentially, separating the posterior vaginal wall from the rectal wall.
The surgeon continues lateral dissection in the pararectal space, freeing the rectum from surrounding tissues, including the levator ani muscle.
The surgeon transects the specimen following total mesorectal excision using an endostapler. The specimen is then retrieved through the umbilical hernia defect after performing a mini-laparotomy.
The surgeon performs vaginal closure using V-lock sutures, securing the tissue edges together.
The surgeon performs an end-to-end coloanal anastomosis using a circular stapler, joining the colon to the anal canal with precise alignment.
The surgeon closes the incisional hernia and places an overlay proline mesh over the defect, securing it in position.
The surgeon performs a diversion loop ileostomy, creating a visible stoma on the abdominal surface to divert bowel contents.
